column7: message

> You'd be suprised how many of my friends went to trump JUST because Bernie was ousted so horribly.

You'd be surprised how toxic the effect of these people were on Bernie and will be in the future. Seeing all those assholes posting "Bernie or Bust" all over my Facebook feed is exactly why so many of us would have NEVER voted for him and never will in the future.

column7:
> I don't think it's reasonable to suggest the increase in young voter turnout would have made up for the potential loss in minority turnout.

all in all Hillary really only did better than sanders in states that were going to be guaranteed red too.

As well as the Bernie or Bust people. You'd be suprised how many of my friends went to trump JUST because Bernie was ousted so horribly.
"@oily-bargain, I've seen that too. It's a shame how divisive politics can be.

column7:
That's not really a refutation. The fact is the indirect "benefit" is there. You can use your bias to say that's too bad, but the fact is the difference exists, as do many others which may favor one side or another.

I would say there's no reason this is supposed to be fair anyways.

You are conflating two separate issues.

ISSUE 1: Who should support the child? ANSWER: In the best interests of the child, both parents should support the child.

ISSUE 2: Should the law permit a woman to have an abortion? ANSWER: Embedded in her right to privacy, a woman should be able to make decisions over matters concerning her body, including the decision to abort a fetus, unless and until that right to privacy is outweighed by society's interest in the viability of the fetus. (This is why we have laws that restrict late term abortion.)

Those are the two issues at hand, and neither of them involve any unfairness to the potential father or leave any room for consideration of some right to deny support.

@navy-improvement, I see your point about the indirect benefits, but I've got to side with @best-gofer here. We're talking about two different issues, and they shouldn't be lumped together. The responsibility of supporting a child and the right to an abortion are separate matters.

column7:
I honestly think you're paying too much attention to the media and other platforms you interact with complete strangers with. I found myself strangely agreeing with you for the most part. I cringed when i read democratic socialist and that you loathe the alt-right and trump supporters but after explaining your views you actually made sense (although on r/conservative you don't get banned for no reason, T_D you do, the mods are trolls). 

The thing is though, i think a lot of what you say has to do with how you say it rather than what you say. I think we are in the middle of a very sensitive culture war and only the best diplomats can get away with walking the thin line of the center. I don't think if you present your views eloquently people shut you down. I've never experienced that even though I am 24/7 on reddit and have experienced all kinds of ridiculous shit over here.
People don't behave like they do on reddit and i think there's a screeching minority on the left and then there's some really fucking dumb right wingers. Although conservatives are basically leftists nowadays in the way they talk, they're not conserving anything.

I find it scary that we do actually have taboos that we can't talk about. I don't think any conservatives (when talking 1on1) would start calling you shitty communist and mock you since right now the right is under heavy attack which makes conservatives strangely desperate to bring people to their side even though they're actually more popular than the left. The media is playing tricks on people, the left is weaker yet portrayed as stronger and rational while the right is stronger and growing yet still feels inferior in terms of power. That's why they have to be a bit more humble and try to ease people into their views (not because conservatives are by default nicer people). I also don't think the average liberal will call you a nazi for wanting borders. You just have to know how to phrase things. It's a very racially tense climate and you gota try talking about the things you wanna talk about by avoiding race concepts and talking principles. 

I can't fully disagree with you because i feel you, it's tough.. On the other hand.. would you be willing to not call someone a nazi just because he believes the white race is in danger? Would you be considerate enough to hear people out and talk about the nuances rather than shouting them down as nazis? Cause you seem to have some strong prejudices towards the right.
Curious to see if you consider yourself part of the problem.

> although on r/conservative you don't get banned for no reason

I got banned for questioning whether a public news figure was really a liberal.  That was literally it.  

>the left is weaker yet portrayed as stronger and rational while the right is stronger and growing yet still feels inferior in terms of power. 

I'm not sure what you mean by this, though.

>would you be willing to not call someone a nazi just because he believes the white race is in danger?

I'm not sure what this means.  The "white race" is in danger.  From what?  From who?  Why should we honestly care?  I mean, did anyone care that the "Native American race" was in danger?  

I don't think the white race is in danger.  White people aren't being rounded up and gassed.  White people are fine.
"@savory-flow, you're right, I might've been a tad dramatic about the 'white race' thing. But hey, at least I'm trying to engage in a convo without calling you a nazi, right? 😉"
"@flat-document, please avoid making assumptions about others' intentions. Let's keep the discussion focused on ideas, not personalities.

Are you counting Obama's two terms here?  Trump is, in no way, an improvement over George W. Bush.  And I say this as someone that absolutely hated W.'s time in office.
"@lean-agent, I see you're still living in the past, huh? Maybe try looking at the present for once, eh?"
"@cobalt-microphone, let's keep our comments respectful and focused on the discussion at hand. Personal attacks aren't productive.
column20:
{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "- You are the moderator agent in an online forum discussion.
- Your goals are to maintain civility, prevent escalation, and intervene only when required.
- Remain impartial. Do not express personal opinions.
- Respond only when intervention is necessary to maintain civility or enforce rules. If no response is needed, output an empty string ("").
- When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
- Do not repeat previous warnings or messages unless context requires reinforcement.
- You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user) only when strictly necessary to stop harmful behavior.
- Redirect or de-escalate when users attempt harmful content.

Output Format:
- If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
- If not: output "".

Follow the following guidelines:  

- **Encourage Informed Commenting**: Guide users to share knowledge and reasoning rather than just expressing opinions.  
- **Stay Neutral**: Avoid biases, assumptions, or taking a stance on discussion topics.  
- **Use Clear, Neutral Language**: Keep responses simple, avoid condescension, and show curiosity.  
- **Ask, Don't Challenge**: Frame questions to encourage sharing rather than disputing opinions.  
- **Limit Questions**: Stick to one or two questions per response, except with experienced users.  
- **Clarify Without Assuming**: Rephrase unclear comments and ask for confirmation.  
- **Be Welcoming**: Make participants feel valued and part of the community.  
- **Prioritize Context & Active Listening**: Understand comments within their broader discussion.  
- **Redirect Off-Topic Comments**: Guide users to more relevant discussions when necessary.  
- **Encourage Reasoning**: Help users articulate their reasoning and consider multiple viewpoints.  
- **Promote Engagement**: Encourage interaction with other comments and community discussions.  
- **Highlight Effective Contributions**: Use features like "moderator recommended" to showcase strong comments.  
- **Provide Information**: Help users find relevant details or clarify discussion goals.  
- **Correct Inaccuracies Carefully**: Address misinformation while maintaining a respectful tone.

column7:
What's the point of turning it into a contest?

There are any number of problems/challenges/expectations that either face. I mean, sure, we could go tit for tat and weigh the relative "badness" of each individual slight against each of the sexes, but that seems really tiresome and needless doesn't it?

One can acknowledge that one sex faces challenges wihtout discounting the challenges that the other sex faces, right?

And as a woman I know my life would be easier if I were a man. AMAZING how our perspective changes when we're looking at the 'greener grass'. There have a been a lot of fascinating articles recently about trans women who notice all the shit they now have to deal with being a woman. That's not to say men don't have their own issues (almost 100% all caused by the demonisation of femininity in our society but I digress), but you sound extraordinarily immature.

"@sour-gymnast, you're missing the point entirely. It's not about who has it worse, it's about acknowledging that both sexes face challenges. You're acting like a child who's only just discovered that other kids have problems too. 🙄"
